speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you can slate it verbally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 11th, 1996. I am interviewing Alfred Winter in his home in Norwalk, Connecticut, United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's in English, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: This interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SWEDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my German is non-existent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I didn't say that in German. I said that in Swedish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Well, [INAUDIBLE] me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred Winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-N-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-F-R-E-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as-- as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birthday?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are, Alfred?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell the city and country of your birth and spell them, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Korschenbroich, K-O-R-S-C-H-E-N-B-R-O-I-C-H, in a government district of Dusseldorf, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What large city is your hometown near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monchengladbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this the city you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I grow in the town where I was born, grow up, but I went to school in the city, to high school later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six children, one girl and five boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was Greta. Then was Kurt, Bruno, Eric, myself, and Herbert.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything special about the five brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm a twin, and so was Eric.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius Winter, and my mother's was Henrietta. Henrietta. Her maiden name was Schwarz, S-C-H-W-A-R-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your house, Alfred, what your house looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a 10-room house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family have servants?

speaker: interviewee
text: One live-in maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a typical day like in your life growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was six years old, I went to grammar school for five days a week because since Jewish children would not attend the sixth day, on the Sabbath, we had the day off, we would go to synagogue. Of course, the town where I was living was Catholic, and there were five Jewish families, but Jewish children would not attend school which is in Germany six days a week. Later on when I went to high school, I had to go six days a week to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large backyard where your brothers and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a large backyard. We had quite a bit of land. My father was a cattle dealer so I grew up just like being on a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of the games you played with your brothers and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hardly, because we played soccer and handball, something we don't play over here in the United States, and some other games. And then we played cards and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was also the cantor in the town. It was a strictly kosher house, but otherwise they were conservative. My father did not do any business on the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a rabbi that gave services at the synagogue in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The rabbi only came when there was a wedding or a funeral or something special. He came from [GERMAN]. His name was Dr. Gelles, G-E-L-L-ES.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relationships with the Gentiles in your town? Did you have friendships?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before 1933, there was no difference. Since my father was also the founder of the so-called [GERMAN], which was the sport club, and he belonged to the Veterans Association from World War I. And there was no difference between Jews and Gentile before Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember playing with Gentile children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit in their homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: In their homes, and they came in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior to 1935, I belonged to the sport club, which was Gentile and Jews mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a club your father started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you did at the sports club?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly gymnastics on handbar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start to learn any trades with your father? Did you help him after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we work in the garden. We learned everything what has to do with the garden, planting vegetables, and we were self-sufficient like a farmer because there was so much land. In the harvest we were helping, and since my father was a cattle dealer, we needed a lot of hay, and so we harvested it and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories do you have of your mother, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few because she died in 1925 of cancer, but my father married later on the sister of my mother, who was a widow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother was in the house, what do you remember about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was six years old, and she spent maybe a year in the hospital. So I remember that we went to the hospital nearly every day after school. That's all I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember holidays in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every holiday we went to the synagogue, and we were specially dressed in good clothing, and so were all the Jews in the town. And we came together because it was just like one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What literature was around your house? Do you remember newspapers or books?

speaker: interviewee
text: The local newspaper, and we had [INAUDIBLE], Theodor Storm, and [INAUDIBLE], which was-- had written all the stories about American Indians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those were some of your favorite books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and history.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler started to come to power, what are your recollections? of that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1932 already in high school, we had already swastikas painted on our desks, and some teacher told my father they should take us out of school because we had no chance to go ahead. Some teachers were already anti-Semitic, and we were getting poor marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the types of school you went to up until this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was what you call [GERMAN], similar like a gymnasium. Gymnasium they were learning as the major language Latin, and we were learning French and English instead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your memories of high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very disciplined, and you had to pass a test before you even could get into high school. And your parents had to pay for it. And if you would flunk out in the third year in high school, you had to leave high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a public high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler came to power, how did that change your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was out of high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father took me in his business to learn the cattle business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also attend any kind of trade school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did, for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What years were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: '33 and '34. And I was the only Jewish boy in that class, and most of the time I was disregarded by the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of your feelings about that disregard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were teaching [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they call in German Rassenkunde. The Jews were second class citizens. They were different and everything. And some teachers were good. They would not make any difference, or they would disregard you completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this-- this business school right in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came home evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] School was only in the afternoons three days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you helped-- did you help with your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the rise of Nazism perceived in your family? Was it discussed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was discussed when everyone said I'll leave, they will not last long. Because the government in Germany during the time when I can remember sometimes would not last six months or not longer than a year. Then the government would fall. And they said the same will happen to Hitler and his gang, also. Of course, the Depression was on, a lot of unemployment, and a lot of people were poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the feelings of the town people start to change towards the Jewish families in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: A certain amount did, a certain amount did not. Some in the public offices changed. There were open feelings of anti-Semitism against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the sights and sounds of what that anti-Semitism looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had-- when you went in, you had to wait to talk to them, or they would treat you like dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever other privileges taken away from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not till '35. After that, you could not go in a restaurant anymore. Say in a bar or something, you were insulted and things like that. That started after '35 after the Nuremberg Laws went into effect. Also, I had some sense I was going out with, a Gentile, and I got told not to go anymore because he had sisters, and it would be a question if I will go out with a girl or with a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what other ways did the Nuremberg Laws affect your life and the life of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Jews welcomed the Nuremberg Laws because there was a lot of intermarriage going on in Germany, and they said it's good. It will stop the intermarriages in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other reactions to the Nuremberg Laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, not. Later on, yes. We found out we were second class citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening in your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to leave Germany. But my father did not want to leave Germany because the business was still good, and he was a soldier in the First World War with seven of his brothers. There were eight brothers who were soldiers. Two were killed on the War, and he was 100% German first and Jew second.

speaker: interviewer
text: 100% German first, that's how he considered himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your brothers and sister consider yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. But when we saw what was going on, we wanted to get out of Germany, and we started rebelling. My twin brother was the first one to rebel, and he left Germany. He joined the Foreign Legion, then became a French citizen afterwards because we had relatives in France who made the arrangement when he stayed in the French army. And he got killed in May, 1945, fighting against the Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the rebellion look like with the rest of the members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my other brother, Bruno, he got a-- had an argument with an SS-- member of the SS-- and he got accused of so-called Rassenschande, and he had to leave Germany illegally in 1936. And he had to go over the border by the night not to be arrested by the Gestapo. And he went to Chile. Santiago Chile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the crime that he was accused of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rassenschande, even though he had no date with a German girl. He had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the crime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what other ways did these laws affect your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some farmers who owed my father money would not pay up anymore. Even if you would take them to court, the court was against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the Nazi presence felt in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say after '35 when the Nuremberg Laws went in effect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe them on the streets or their headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first time we saw in 1933 when the [INAUDIBLE]. Then we had two members of the Nazi party standing in front of our house that nobody should go in and out. That was in 1933 and April 1. After that, we didn't hear or see anything till 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you see and hear in 1935?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we openly got insulted as dirty Jews and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there prohibitions about where you could go in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could not go in a bar anymore later on and things like that, but otherwise, there were no prohibitions. Not at that time. Not in '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the-- where were the Nazis housed in your town? Where were their headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a street nearby, and we didn't hear and see much of them, but when they marched with the-- through the streets and like that, the Jews made sure they were not on the streets and they were in the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any rumors of other parts of the country or things going on in other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we heard rumors about concentration camps, and we know that some Gentiles were arrested, too. Were communists and things like that, that they went on trial and went to jail, and things like that. We heard about that, and also that some Jews got killed in the concentration camps. And they all died of heart attacks or something else, they said, the Germans, but the bodies were not returned. Only they could have the ashes of the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing till 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the pogrom of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house [INAUDIBLE] three rooms. The furniture got smashed, the windows got smashed, and I got arrested. So did my father. And all other [INAUDIBLE] were arrested in town during that time. My brother was-- oldest brother-- was in Westphalia. He got arrested, and he got shipped out to Oranienburg concentration camp. My father was sent to a prison nearby, and since I was still considered a minor they let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now, Alfred?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, I was 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were released?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was released. And then I had to replace the windows and everything what was smashed. So the Jews had to fix up the houses, and you could not get any outside help to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is left to home with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my sister, and my youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your father remarried at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he remarried in 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what your new mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fanny, and she was the sister of my first mother. She was a widow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was your father away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was away close to three months, and they let him go under the condition that he would sell his property. And the Nazi government set a certain price for the property, and the money went in a bank account which we could not touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much property was involved in his cattle business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house, the barns, and some grazing land on-- on some farmland where we had a big garden, food garden where we were planting vegetables. We were self-sufficient at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date is it when your father returns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of '39, and he had to sign everything over. The Nazis bought it for 6,500 marks, which was confiscated. I got it back later on when I sold it for over 40,000 marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family once your father returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to get out of Germany, and we could not have any business anymore because all Jewish businesses were closed. And I was forced to work for a farmer, and we would get minimum wage. And then I had to-- a short time later, I had to work on the Autobahn, which was very difficult. I had to go to Dusseldorf by train, and I had to work there six days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken, transported, to this forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it began in 1939 when your father returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of your brothers with you on the forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, he got a phony paper that he could go to South America. He got released on the concentration camp, and he had to do forced labor in a swamp nearby. The Germans were drying out a swamp. And so was my youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what this forced labor was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could go home every day, but we had to do a certain amount of work every day for minimum wage. And there was also a special tax for Jews because they were not-- not member of the Army, and there was an extra 15% tax taken off the salary for that purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were supplies in your house like food and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no problem with that till the war started. Then it was more difficult to get everything. But since we were still self-sufficient with most of the things, we had no problems there. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remained in your own house during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we remained in our own house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were things out on the street, like when you were going to the forced labor? Did you see differences?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were disregarded and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in general, we in the town had no problem, but in the cities there were problems. Jews were assaulted on the street and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything personally happen to you during this forced labor time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we got also a visa to go to Chile. And we made the booking and everything, but the war broke out. We could not get out of Germany anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your daily life, who were you in contact with during this forced labor, and where was it located?

speaker: interviewee
text: First it was in Dusseldorf. Then I had to work for a while in the swamp Only Jews were working there. And then later on, I had to work for a German company reclaiming and recycling. And we were treated as second class citizen. We had to do what they told us, and you could not open your mouth and answer back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of you on these work details?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first work detail was a contractor who was a member of the SS, and what we had to do, we had to do some road building in a swamp and had to take the rubble from the synagogues, which were destroyed in [INAUDIBLE], and we had to build road from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your work supervised, and how were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say as long as you did not step out of line, you were treated like a human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food provided while you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had rationing cards, and some stores had signs on it, Jews will not be served. Other stores, we could get what we had on the ration card. But in general, we had no shortage of food till 1941. Then it was a different ballgame After we had to wear the Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember coming home what your thoughts and dreams were each night after this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can we get out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued to try to find ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but there was-- in the beginning, some people could get out. The Germans let you get out till somewhere in 1941, people who could go to the United States, who had relatives who paid for the fare and things like that. Some could go to Switzerland or Sweden, but very few because Switzerland, we know, was very anti-Semitic during that time. Sweden, Norway, and Denmark were not anti-Semitic, and Holland was not either, but the Germans took Holland in 1940. It was a different ballgame after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how long were you at Dusseldorf in the forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: For nearly a year. Then I worked for nearly a year in a swamp. And then I worked for the reclaiming and recycling company for nearly a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, the change after Rosh Hashanah in 1941 when we have to wear the Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Star of David made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cloth, yellow, with the Star of David printed in black, and then the German word for Jew, 'Jude,' was printed in, and you had to wear on the left-hand side of the vest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to have it on all layers of your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the outer clothing only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you went outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we were restricted. We had to be in the house at 8 o'clock in the evening. You could not go out, and you could not leave the town unless you had a permit from the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your family was still all together at home at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you wore the Star of David, what was the next action towards your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next action was that in December beginning-- no, end of November, 1941, we got notified we would be deported to Riga, Latvia, for to do work over there. And at the same time, my parents got told, since they're over 60 years of age, if they want to they can stay back, but my parents decided to go with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that notification looked like, what form it came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did not come in a form. We were told to come to town hall to the police department there, and the fellow who was in charge there and also representing the Gestapo read us that we would be deported. That we would be able to take 40 pounds of clothing along, plus blankets, and our food cards would be validated for six weeks so we could take additional food along. We could take eating utensils along, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your family started to get ready, can you describe what your house was like during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we got the notice, my brother and I myself wanted to get married because we both were engaged. My oldest brother got permission to get married because there was one official in the town hall where he was living was very friendly to the Jews despite the Nazis, and he got the Gestapo gave the permit for one to be married. And they could get married within three days in Germany. Otherwise, it takes two weeks to get the permit. So they got married on December 6, 1941, and four days later, we got deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your application was denied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Gestapo denied it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date you were set to leave for the deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 10, a policeman took us to [NON-ENGLISH], on a train to Dusseldorf, to the cattle house of the slaughterhouse. We were received by the Gestapo and treated like cattle. Everyone was frisk, and anything what the Germans thought was good for the war effort or things what they figured we didn't need was confiscated.
